Introduction
I still remember the first time I wrote a React component. It was 2023, and the idea that I could build a dynamic, interactive UI with just JavaScript felt revolutionary. No more jQuery spaghetti. No more wrestling with server-rendered templates. React gave me the freedom to compose interfaces like LEGO blocks, to think in components, and to build exactly what I wanted without a framework telling me where to put things.
And for a while, that was enough. I loved React’s flexibility. I loved that I could choose my own router, my own state management, my own build tool. But over time, that freedom started to feel like a burden.
Every new project began the same way: scaffold a React app, configure Webpack, set up React Router, add a state library, figure out server-side rendering, optimize images, configure SEO metadata, set up TypeScript, add linting, and on and on. By the time I wrote my first component, I had already spent hours on plumbing. And when it came time to ship to production? The real headaches began. Performance budgets, SEO concerns, hydration mismatches, image optimization pipelines — problems that had nothing to do with my application logic but everything to do with whether it would succeed in the real world.
I was building great UIs. But I wasn’t building great applications.
The Turning Point
I first encountered Next.js in mid 2024, but I’ll be honest — I didn’t get it at first. “It’s just React with a router,” I thought. “Why would I give up my carefully curated stack for another opinionated framework?”
The learning curve was real. Next.js wasn’t just React with routing bolted on. It had opinions about file structure, about how pages should be organized, about where server logic lived versus client logic. It felt restrictive at first, almost like I was losing the flexibility I valued so much in React.
But then something clicked.
I rebuilt my own portfolio. I already had one — plain HTML, CSS, and vanilla JavaScript, hand-coded and hosted on GitHub Pages. I rebuilt it in Next.js, with Tailwind and shadcn/ui for the interface and Framer Motion for the animations, and had it live within a weekend. What struck me wasn’t how much more I did — it was how much less I had to. Routing that I used to wire up by hand now just followed the file structure. Images optimized themselves without me touching a build pipeline. Features I’d never gotten around to in the old version came together in an afternoon instead of a week, because the framework was handling the scaffolding I used to build myself. The pages loaded instantly. And when I finally ran Lighthouse, the scores were staggering. Nothing I’d ever come close to before with my hand-rolled projects.
What stood out immediately wasn’t any single feature. It was the cohesion. Every part of Next.js felt designed to work with every other part. The framework wasn’t fighting me — it was anticipating problems I hadn’t even thought about yet.
The Features That Changed Everything
App Router
The App Router wasn’t just a new routing system — it was a rethinking of how web applications should be architected.
Nested layouts meant I could define a sidebar once and have it persist across route changes without remounting. Loading states were declarative. Error boundaries were built in. The mental model shifted from “pages as entry points” to “routes as a tree of components,” and suddenly complex applications felt manageable again.
Server Components
React Server Components were the feature I didn’t know I needed. The idea that I could fetch data directly inside a component on the server without shipping that logic to the client was a revelation. No more prop drilling through loading states. No more useEffect waterfalls. Components became self-contained units of functionality, not just presentation.
My bundle sizes dropped. My initial page loads got faster. And the boundary between server and client became a design decision, not a technical limitation.
Nested Layouts
Before nested layouts, every route change in a single-page app meant potential layout glitches. The sidebar flickered. The navigation remounted. State was lost. With Next.js App Router, layouts compose naturally. A dashboard sidebar stays mounted while the main content swaps. State persists. Animations work without hacks.
This sounds small, but it’s the difference between an application that feels* fast and one that is fast.
Route Handlers
Replacing API routes with Route Handlers felt like a natural evolution. Instead of a separate /pages/api directory, handlers live alongside the routes they serve. A form submission handler lives in the same directory as the form component. The mental overhead of “where does this logic go?” disappeared.
Image Optimization
I used to spend hours configuring Sharp, setting up CDN pipelines, and writing responsive image components. Next.js’s <Image> component handles formats, sizes, lazy loading, and blur placeholders out of the box. I stopped thinking about image optimization because the framework stopped making it my problem.
Metadata API
SEO used to be an afterthought. Next.js’s Metadata API makes SEO a first-class concern. Open Graph tags, Twitter cards, canonical URLs — all type-safe, all server-rendered, all automatic. For client projects where search visibility is business-critical, this alone justifies the framework choice.
Built-in TypeScript Support
TypeScript in Next.js isn’t an add-on; it’s the default. The framework generates types for your routes, your metadata, your API responses. Autocomplete works for dynamic routes. Refactoring is safe. The “any” types that used to creep into my React projects simply don’t have a place here.
Performance Out of the Box
I used to chase performance. Now I inherit it. Code splitting is automatic. Prefetching is intelligent. Static generation happens at build time for pages that don’t need dynamic data. The framework makes the right choice by default, and I only override when I have a specific reason.
Real Projects
Theory is satisfying, but production is where frameworks prove themselves. Here are two projects where Next.js changed not just how I built, but what I was capable of building.
My Portfolio
My personal site was the first Next.js project I shipped. It’s a simple portfolio with a blog, but the details mattered. Images load with blur-up placeholders. Blog posts are statically generated at build time. The Lighthouse score is 100 across the board — not because I optimized it to death, but because I didn’t have to.
A Music Streaming Application
This app was where Next.js really proved its worth. A streaming app has competing needs: real-time audio playback (client-side), dynamic playlist data (server-side), and SEO for discoverability (static where possible). With Next.js, I could use Server Components for playlist metadata and search results, while keeping the audio player as a client component with its own state and lifecycle. The boundaries were clear, the architecture was clean, and the performance was better than anything I’d built before.
Internal Tools
Not every project is customer-facing. I’ve built dashboards, admin panels, and reporting tools with Next.js, and the developer experience pays dividends here too. Route handlers make API integration trivial. Server Components keep sensitive data off the client. And the same framework I use for public sites works perfectly for internal tools — no context switching, no separate stack.
The lesson across all these projects wasn’t about any specific feature. It was that Next.js let me focus on the product instead of the plumbing. The framework handles the decisions that don’t differentiate my work, so I can spend energy on the ones that do.
Lessons Learned
Next.js isn’t magic, and it took some hard lessons to use it well.
Server-side rendering isn’t always the answer
Early on, I defaulted to Server Components for everything. Why not? They’re faster, they’re secure, they’re the future. But I learned that interactivity requires client components, and forcing server rendering where user interaction is central creates friction. Now I start with Server Components and promote to client components only when I need interactivity, state, or browser APIs. It’s a deliberate choice, not a default.
Keep client components minimal
The temptation is to wrap everything in use “client” the moment you need a click handler. But client components ship JavaScript. The more you use, the heavier your bundles. I’ve learned to push logic as far up the tree as possible, keeping leaf components as server-rendered HTML and only hydrating the interactive islands.
Understand the rendering model
The biggest mistake I see developers make is treating Next.js like React with extra steps. It’s not. The rendering model, i.e. static, dynamic, server, client, is the core architecture. Deciding where code runs is as important as deciding what it does. Once I internalized this, my applications became simpler and faster simultaneously.
Why I Still Choose Next.js
Some years after my first Next.js project, the landscape is crowded with alternatives. New frameworks emerge monthly. But I keep coming back to Next.js for five reasons:
Performance — Not just fast loads, but predictable performance. The defaults are right, and the escape hatches are there when I need them.
Developer experience — The feedback loop is tight. Error messages are helpful. The documentation is exceptional. I spend less time configuring and more time creating.
SEO — For anything that needs to be found on the internet, Next.js removes the guesswork. Metadata, sitemaps, structured data — all first-class.
Scalability— The same codebase that serves a marketing site can grow into a full application. I don’t outgrow the framework; it grows with me.
Deployment flexibility— And this is where my journey gets interesting. Next.js doesn’t force me into a specific hosting platform. Static exports, serverless functions, Docker containers, standalone builds — the framework adapts to my infrastructure, not the other way around.
Conclusion
React taught me to think in components. Next.js taught me to think in applications. The shift wasn’t just technical — it was philosophical. I stopped assembling stacks and started building products. I stopped optimizing by default and started shipping by default.
If you’re still hand-rolling your React setup, I understand the appeal. There’s pride in knowing every bolt in your machine. But there’s also liberation in trusting a framework that has solved problems you haven’t encountered yet — and probably never will.
Next.js didn’t just change how I build web applications. It changed what I believe is possible to build in a weekend, what I can promise a client, and what “production-ready” means to me.
In my next article, I’ll show how I deploy a production-ready Next.js Standalone application to DirectAdmin/cPanel with CloudLinux Passenger, proving that modern frameworks aren’t limited to cloud platforms. Because the best application in the world is worthless if you can’t ship it where you need to. And sometimes, where you need to isn’t Vercel, AWS, or a container orchestration platform. Sometimes it’s the shared hosting your client already has — and Next.js can handle that too.
